'''Trent Barrett''' (born 18 November 1977) is an Australian professional [[rugby league]] football coach who is currently the assistant coach of the [[Brisbane Broncos]] and a former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s.

He was previously the head coach of the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]] and [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] in the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL). A former [[Australian Kangaroos|Australia]] international and [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] State of Origin representative {{rlp|fe}}, he played during the 1990s and 2000s for the [[Illawarra Steelers]] before they formed a joint venture with [[St. George Dragons|St. George]] to create the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]], with whom he won the 2000 [[Dally M Medal]]. Barrett also had a two-season spell in the [[Super League]] with England's [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] and was named in 2007's [[Super League Dream Team]] before finishing his career back in Australia with the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]]. After co-coaching the St George Illawarra under 20s side and coaching the [[Country New South Wales rugby league team]] for four seasons Barrett started coaching in the NRL with the Sea Eagles.

==Background==
Barrett was born in [[Temora, New South Wales]] on 18 November 1977. He is a cousin of [[Australian rules football]]ers [[Luke Breust]] and [[Jake Barrett (footballer)|Jake Barrett]],<ref>{{Cite news |last=Matic |first=Joshua |date=2013-08-02 |title=AFL prodigy has plenty of kicking cousins |language=en |work=The Age |url=https://www.theage.com.au/sport/afl/afl-prodigy-has-plenty-of-kicking-cousins-20130802-2r54o.html |url-status=live |access-date=2020-11-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201126225354/https://www.theage.com.au/sport/afl/afl-prodigy-has-plenty-of-kicking-cousins-20130802-2r54o.html |archive-date=2020-11-26}}</ref> NRL footballer [[Liam Martin]]<ref>{{Cite news |last=Walshaw |first=Nick |title='Nothing felt real for six months': Tragedy inspires young Panther |work=The Daily Telegraph|location=London |url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/nrl-2020-penrith-panthers-liam-martin-driven-by-private-pain/news-story/f5d78ba582e025c075759f08305ecd31 |url-access=subscription |access-date=2020-11-27}}</ref> and Australian water polo player Anthony Martin.

While attending [[St Gregory's College, Campbelltown]], he played for the [[Australia national schoolboys rugby league team|Australian Schoolboys team]] in 1995.<ref>{{Cite web |title=SportingPulse Homepage for Australian Secondary Schools Rugby League |url=http://www.sportingpulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424 |access-date=10 October 2008 |publisher=SportingPulse}}</ref>

==Playing career==
===1990s===
{{BLP unreferenced section|date=November 2024}}
Barrett started his professional career in the [[1996 ARL season]] with the [[Illawarra Steelers]].

Within two years he had been selected to play his first [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] match.

Barrett was the stand-out player of the [[1998 Illawarra Steelers season]], top scoring with 18 tries and winning the BHP Medal as player of the year.

His first season at the new joint-venture of the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]] was marred by controversy as the incessant media hype over an alleged feud with [[Anthony Mundine]] and whether by playing out of position at [[Rugby league positions#Scrum-half|half-back]] was affecting his form. He played at halfback in St. George Illawarra's [[1999 NRL Grand Final]] loss to Melbourne.

===2000s===
With the shock departure of Mundine from St. George Illawarra in 2000, Barrett was allowed to move back to his original position of {{rlp|fe}} and he received the [[Dally M Medal]] in 2000.{{citation needed|date=November 2024}}

At the end of the [[2001 NRL season]], he went on the [[2001 Kangaroo tour]].

He scored 6 tries in 20 games in the [[2002 NRL season]].

In 2003, while Barrett was captain of St George Illawarra, [[Nathan Brown (rugby league, born 1973)|Nathan Brown]] who was the coach at the time famously slapped Barrett across the face in a tense sideline spray alongside [[Lance Thompson]]. Brown admitted many years later it was one of the biggest regrets in his coaching career but said he had moved on from the incident.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Jones |first=Quinn |date=2016-04-18 |title=No jusitfying infamous Barrett slap: Brown |work=Wide World of Sports |url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/2016/04/18/19/18/nathan-brown-admits-trent-barrett-slap-was-biggest-regret-of-his-career |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160811110817/https://wwos.nine.com.au/2016/04/18/19/18/nathan-brown-admits-trent-barrett-slap-was-biggest-regret-of-his-career |archive-date=2016-08-11}}</ref>

Following a Round 12, 2006 game against the [[Newcastle Knights]], Barrett received a grade-five striking charge on Newcastle Knights' [[Brian Carney (rugby league)|Brian Carney]].{{citation needed|date=November 2024}} The incident initially went unnoticed throughout the entire weekend but it is understood a [[Nine Network|Channel Nine]] producer picked up the incident. Barrett was suspended for six matches, ruling him out of [[Dally M Medal]] contention.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}}
[[File:Trent Barrett.jpg|thumb|right|Barrett playing for Wigan in 2008]]
Barrett had two years left on his St. George Illawarra Dragons contract, but a get-out clause enabled him to quit the club for a move to England.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}} He signed a three-year deal with the [[Wigan Warriors]] on 4 May 2006, despite their position at the bottom of the [[Super League]] and the threat of relegation to National League Division 1.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}}

Barrett made his Wigan Warriors début in a 16–10 defeat against Warrington at the JJB Stadium on 9 February 2007. In October 2007, Barrett was named as the Rugby League Players' Player of the Year but missed out on the "Man of Steel" although he had been tipped to be named.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2006-10-09 |title=Barrett no Man of Steel |work=League HQ |url=http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/news/barrett-no-man-of-steel/2007/10/09/1191695884817.html |url-status=dead |access-date=2007-10-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080610073436/http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/news/barrett-no-man-of-steel/2007/10/09/1191695884817.html |archive-date=2008-06-10}}</ref> [[James Roby]] of St Helens received the award.
[[File:Trent Barrett Cronulla Sharks.jpg|thumb|right|Barrett playing for the Sharks in 2010]]
Following a release from the final year of his three-year contract with Wigan, Barrett signed with the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] in June 2008, on a two-year deal, commencing in 2009.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2008-06-05 |title=Barrett signs up with Sharks |work=Fox Sports News |agency=Australian Associated Press |url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,23815084-23214,00.html |url-status=dead |access-date=2008-06-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110522105017/http://www.foxsports.com.au/league/nrl-premiership/barrett-signs-up-with-sharks/story-e6frf3uc-1111116548758 |archive-date=2011-05-22}}</ref>

He earned the five-eighth spot for the [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales team]] in the 2nd [[2009 State of Origin series|State of Origin match of 2009]]. In his Origin comeback, Barrett was charged following a reckless tackle on [[Queensland State of Origin team|Queensland]]'s [[Greg Inglis]], and as a result was suspended for two club matches.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}} It did not, however, deter him from playing in the third and final Origin match in which he set up several tries as New South Wales defeated Queensland in the dead rubber, Queensland having won the series with victory three weeks prior. Barrett announced his retirement on 22 July 2010.{{Citation needed|date=November 2024}}

=== Representative games ===
*"Club:" Played 199 games for the [[Illawarra Steelers]] and the St George Illawarra Dragons
*''Country:'' Played for [[Country New South Wales rugby league team|Country]] in [[City vs Country Origin]]
*''State of Origin:'' Played 11 games in total for [[New South Wales]]
*''International:'' Played 13 tests to date for Australia including the 2000 World Cup

=== Awards ===
*''Dally M:'' [[Dally M Medal|Dally M]] ''Player of the Year'' in 2000
* Super League Players' Player of the Year 2007

==Coaching career==
On 5 April 2014, Barrett was announced as the new head coach of the [[Italy national rugby league team|Italian national rugby league team]] when previous coach [[Carlo Napolitano]] announced his departure after Italy's [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]] campaign.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2014-04-05 |title=Rugby League: Trent Barrett named Italy's new head coach |publisher=Sky Sports |location=United Kingdom |url=http://www1.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/15323/9251204/rugby-league-trent-barrett-named-italys-new-head-coach |url-status=live |access-date=2014-04-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140406113940/http://www1.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/15323/9251204/rugby-league-trent-barrett-named-italys-new-head-coach |archive-date=2014-04-06}}</ref> He coached Italy in their World Cup Qualifying matches, while [[Paul Broadbent]] coached Italy in the minor European Championship competitions.

In 2016, Barrett became the head coach for [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]]. In Barrett's first season as Manly coach the club finished 13th on the table and missed the finals. The following season, Barrett took Manly to a 6th-place finish on the table and qualified for the finals but were eliminated in controversial circumstances against Penrith in week one of the finals.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2017/09/10/panthers-tyrone-peachey-on-decisive-try-against-sea-eagles-finals/|title=Peachey on decisive try: I couldn't tell|website=nrl.com|date=9 September 2017 }}</ref>

In 2018, Manly and Barrett suffered a horror year on and off the field with the club finishing second last and narrowly avoiding the wooden spoon. The club was also plagued with infighting and there were reported disagreements between Barrett and the Manly owners over lack of chairs and whose responsibility it was to provide them. Trent eventually supplied the chairs himself but the effort was too late to save the season.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2018-10-03 |title=NRL 2018: Manly Sea Eagles demands Trent Barrett serve 12 month notice until July as contract stand-off heats up |language=en-AU |work=Fox Sports News |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/manly-want-trent-barrett-to-serve-12-month-notice-until-july/news-story/a9030fef2f70c78b52fa807805b66a9a |url-status=live |access-date=2018-10-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181017042923/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/manly-want-trent-barrett-to-serve-12-month-notice-until-july/news-story/a9030fef2f70c78b52fa807805b66a9a?nk=8cdd356a625689f3bbe5a6c7872b016f-1539735422 |archive-date=2018-10-17}}</ref>

On 22 October 2018, Manly announced that former two-time premiership-winning coach [[Des Hasler]] would be appointed as the new Manly head coach for 2019 despite the fact that Barrett still had 12 months remaining on his contract and was not officially terminated by the club.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Whaley |first=Pamela |date=2018-10-22 |title=Manly Sea Eagles appoint Des Hasler as coach for 2019: Five burning questions, Trent Barrett, John Cartwright |work=Fox Sports |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/sea-eagles/what-about-trent-barrett-five-burning-questions-for-des-haslers-sea-eagles/news-story/9800324bdf97b4718e2b43f3c04427d9 |url-status=live |access-date=2018-10-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181022225416/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/sea-eagles/what-about-trent-barrett-five-burning-questions-for-des-haslers-sea-eagles/news-story/9800324bdf97b4718e2b43f3c04427d9 |archive-date=2018-10-22}}</ref>

On 6 August 2019, Barrett spoke to the media and said that in some way he deserved credit for Manly's form reversal in 2019 which saw the club go from finishing second last in 2018 to contenders the following season.  Barrett said "I’d actually like to think I left them well-educated, A few of the boys I got there like Siro (Curtis Sironen) were coming out of reserve grade, No one else wanted [[Moses Suli]]. He’d been punted by the [[Wests Tigers]] and the Bulldogs, Tommy Turbo had only played five games when I started".

Barrett's comments were later slammed online by the daughter of [[Bob Fulton]], Kirstie Fulton took to [[Facebook]] and wrote "History will not be rewritten, no way. I sit back and watch and read people take credit for many things and it’s hard to digest at times because I know who has been and continues to be instrumental in driving a lot of the change behind the scenes but I won’t accept this at all. Is he kidding? His arrogance and ego nearly tore the place apart".<ref>{{Cite news |date=2019-08-06 |title=Trent Barrett slammed for taking credit for Manly resurgence |work=Wide World of Sports |url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/nrl/trent-barrett-slammed-for-taking-manly-credit/b84afe28-3562-460c-a6ad-b76fbf73a339/ |url-status=live |access-date=2019-08-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190807100904/https://wwos.nine.com.au/nrl/trent-barrett-slammed-for-taking-manly-credit/b84afe28-3562-460c-a6ad-b76fbf73a339 |archive-date=2019-08-07}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-08-05 |title=Former Sea Eagles coach Trent Barrett wants credit for Manly's resurgence under Des Hasler |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/former-sea-eagles-coach-trent-barrett-wants-credit-for-manlys-resurgence-under-des-hasler/news-story/eea5fd7fd44cf7d225a3820a66205548/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190807100909/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/former-sea-eagles-coach-trent-barrett-wants-credit-for-manlys-resurgence-under-des-hasler/news-story/eea5fd7fd44cf7d225a3820a66205548 |archive-date=2019-08-07 |access-date=2019-08-07 |website=Fox Sports}}</ref>

On 16 August 2019, it was announced that Barrett would be returning to Penrith as assistant coach to [[Ivan Cleary]] beginning in 2020.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-08-16 |title=Exiled Manly coach lands a job at rival club |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/panthers/trent-barretts-back-exiled-manly-coach-lands-a-job-at-rival-club/news-story/755b6e889029e9dda465c65bab1ba6e9/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190816094944/https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/panthers/trent-barretts-back-exiled-manly-coach-lands-a-job-at-rival-club/news-story/755b6e889029e9dda465c65bab1ba6e9 |archive-date=2019-08-16 |website=Fox Sports}}</ref>

On 23 July 2020, it was announced that Barrett would be the head coach of Canterbury-Bankstown from 2021 onwards on a three-year deal.  Barrett began his head coaching role with an opening round defeat against Newcastle.  In round 2 & 3 of the [[2021 NRL season]], Canterbury were kept scoreless by Penrith and Brisbane losing 28-0 and 24-0 respectively.  This was the first time in the club's history they had been held scoreless in consecutive games.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-03-27/nrl-parramatta-beats-cronulla-brisbane-defeats-bulldogs/100028698/|title=Parramatta unbeaten in NRL after 28-4 win over Cronulla as Brisbane and Warriors enjoy victories|newspaper=ABC News |date=27 March 2021 |publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation}}</ref>

In round 4 of the [[2021 NRL season]], Canterbury were defeated 38-0 by the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] in the traditional Good Friday game. Canterbury became only the second team in the NRL era to lose three straight games without scoring a point after Cronulla who achieved this in the [[2014 NRL season]]. It was also the worst start to a season by any team since Glebe in the [[1928 NSWRFL season]] who managed to only score 12 points in their first four matches.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/nrl/story/_/id/31181206/nrl-2021-round-4-south-sydney-rabbitohs-consign-canterbury-bulldogs-third-straight-scoreless-game/|title=Rabbitohs shine, Dogs scoreless in third straight game|date=2 April 2021 |publisher=ESPN}}</ref>
In round 7 of the 2021 NRL season, Barrett earned his first win as Canterbury-Bankstown head coach after the club snapped a six-game losing streak to beat [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks|Cronulla-Sutherland]] 18–12.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-04-24/nrl-cowboys-beat-raiders-bulldogs-defeat-sharks/100084696/|title=Cowboys beat Raiders 26-24 for third straight NRL win, Bulldogs defeat Sharks 18-12 for maiden victory|newspaper=ABC News |date=24 April 2021 |publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation}}</ref>

In round 16 of the 2021 NRL season, Canterbury were defeated 66-0 by the [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles|Manly Sea Eagles]]. It was Canterbury's third biggest defeat in their 86-year history.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2021/07/03/saab-and-turbo-hit-top-gear-as-sea-eagles-cruise-rout-66/|title=Saab and Turbo hit top gear as Sea Eagles cruise Rout 66|website=nrl.com|date=3 July 2021 }}</ref> At the end of the [[2021 NRL season]], Canterbury finished last on the table and claimed their sixth [[Wooden Spoon (award)|Wooden Spoon]] after winning only three matches all year.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-2021-st-georgeillawarra-dragons-gold-coast-titans-departing-players-corey-norman-ashley-taylor-tyrone-peachey-transfer-centre-signings/news-story/a6c1cdb196e86219a8075fa120d8ae69/|title=Bulldogs' backline boost as rising star re-signs; Eels hooker seals UK exit: Transfer Centre|website=foxsports.com.au|date=16 September 2021 }}</ref>

On 16 May 2022, Barrett resigned from his role as head coach of the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2022/05/16/trent-barrett-stands-down-as-coach-of-bulldogs/|title=Bulldogs begin search for Barrett replacement after emotional exit|website=nrl.com|date=16 May 2022 }}</ref>
Barrett finished his tenure with the second lowest win percentage of any Canterbury coach since the clubs foundation in 1935 with 15.2 percent, only inaugural head coach [[Tedda Courtney]] had a lower win percentage with 12.5 percent.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/may/16/nrl-canterbury-bulldogs-coach-trent-barrett/|title=Trent Barrett's record speaks for itself as Bulldogs start another NRL rebuild|website=The Guardian|date=16 May 2022 |last1=Tedeschi |first1=Nick }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://7news.com.au/sport/rugby-league/barrett-faces-a-dog-of-a-coaching-record-c-6581276/|title=Barrett faces a dog of a coaching record|website=7news.com.au|date=26 April 2022 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.camdenadvertiser.com.au/story/7740322/familiar-flaws-haunt-barretts-dogs-reign/|title=Familiar flaws haunt Barrett's Dogs reign|website=camdenadvertiser.com.au|date=16 May 2022 }}</ref>

On 18 August 2022, Barrett signed with the [[Parramatta Eels]] as an assistant coach, starting in 2023. On 20 May 2024, Barrett was named interim head coach at the Parramatta club after [[Brad Arthur]] was terminated from the head coaching position. Barrett took over Parramatta with the team sitting 14th on the table.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-news-2024-brad-arthur-sacked-by-parramatta-eels-magic-round-loss-confirmation-replacement/news-story/71bcd76f701e8fd6718a0f5582c82bef/|title='This isn't the end for me': Arthur speaks after brutal sacking as payout figure revealed|website=www.foxsports.com.au}}</ref>
In Barrett's first game as interim head coach, Parramatta would lose 42-26 against the bottom placed [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] team who were missing eight players and only had one previous win to their name before the match.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-05-25/nrl-live-updates-raiders-roosters-sharks-panthers-rabbitohs-eels/103878460/|title=Nicho Hynes calf concern casts doubt on NSW selection as Penrith beat Cronulla 42-0|website=www.abc.net.au}}</ref>
The following week, he earned his first win in charge as Parramatta upset top of the table Cronulla 34-22.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2024/05/30/parra-power-past-sharks-as-stars-return-in-style/|title=Parra power past Sharks as stars return in style|website=www.nrl.com}}</ref>
Barrett would eventually guide Parramatta to a 15th placed finish at the end of the 2024 NRL season.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nine.com.au/sport/nrl/news-2024-the-mole-exclusive-parramatta-eels-season-review-reagan-campbell-gillard-future-20240907-p5k8qj.html/|title=The Mole's end of season review: What went wrong at the Parramatta Eels?|website=www.nine.com.au}}</ref>

In November 2024, Barrett joined the [[Brisbane Broncos]] as an assistant coach,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.broncos.com.au/news/20242/11/04/teo-and-barrett-begin-as-broncos-assistant-coaches/|title=Te’o And Barrett Begin As Broncos Assistant Coaches|publisher=Brisbane Broncos|date=4 November 2024|accessdate=18 October 2025}}</ref> ultimately becoming part of the club's [[2025 NRL Grand Final|2025 premiership winning]] coaching staff.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://au.news.yahoo.com/huge-boost-for-broncos-as-premiership-winner-rejects-offer-to-join-rival-queensland-team-205150172.html|title=Huge boost for Broncos as premiership-winner rejects offer to join rival Queensland team|publisher=Yahoo! Sport|first=Sam|last=Goodwin|date=11 October 2025|accessdate=18 October 2025}}</ref>
